answers.field_label: Initial Received Information And Opt Out
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
I am requesting that my child, Ethan J. Carlson, be opted out of all state-required standardized assessments for the 2025–2026 school year. Over the past several years, I have observed that these high-stakes tests create significant anxiety for him and do not accurately reflect his abilities or growth as a learner.

Ethan has an identified anxiety disorder, and long, timed, computer-based tests have historically led to sleeplessness the night before, stomachaches, and emotional meltdowns in the days leading up to and following the testing window. While his classroom teacher has been very supportive and has tried strategies to reduce stress, the overall environment of “testing season” (practice tests, preparation packets, frequent reminders about the importance of test scores, and modified schedules) still has a strong negative impact on his mental health and general attitude toward school.

Academically, Ethan is a strong reader and does well in math and science when assessed through classroom projects, short quizzes, and teacher-created assessments aligned with what he is actually learning. His report cards, teacher conferences, and work samples give me much more detailed and useful information than a single test score given on one or two days in the spring. I regularly communicate with his teachers and review the feedback they provide through assignments, project rubrics, and narrative comments, and I feel that these measures give us a much clearer and more holistic sense of his progress.

I also have concerns about the amount of instructional time that is lost to test preparation, practice sessions, and administration of the assessments themselves. Ethan has expressed frustration that several weeks of the school year feel focused primarily on testing rather than on hands-on learning, reading for enjoyment, and deeper projects that he genuinely loves (especially science experiments and small-group reading discussions). I would prefer that his time in school be used for meaningful instruction, collaboration, and creative learning activities rather than extensive test prep.

Additionally, I have questions about how the test data are ultimately used. As a parent, I receive a score report months after the exam that provides a general proficiency level, but it does not give specific, actionable feedback that helps me or his teachers support his learning in a targeted way. Given that Ethan already participates in regular classroom assessments and progress monitoring, I do not feel that the statewide test results add enough value to justify the stress and disruption they cause for him.

For these reasons—concerns about Ethan’s mental health and anxiety, the limited instructional value of the tests for him personally, the loss of meaningful instructional time, and the lack of detailed, timely feedback from the score reports—I am formally refusing his participation in the statewide MCA/MTAS Reading, Mathematics, and Science assessments, as well as the ACCESS or Alternate ACCESS for ELLs (if applicable). I understand that this decision may have implications for school accountability reporting and that Ethan may receive a “not proficient” or similar notation in statewide reporting systems. I also understand that this form must be completed each school year in which I choose to opt him out.

I am happy to work collaboratively with his teachers and school administrators to ensure that Ethan is engaged in alternative learning activities during testing times and that his academic progress continues to be monitored through classroom-based assessments and other measures that better fit his needs.
